splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate withdrawal liability to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year or whether they can be selected afterward. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be determined as of the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability about potential liability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, giving plans flexibility to use updated information when calculating withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Trustees of IAM National Pension Fund v. M & K Employee Solutions

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the period of court-ordered conditions beyond the judgment when a person absconds. This affects thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, preserving the court's ability to revoke supervision for violations that occur during the absconding period.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and the term expires according to its stated duration.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: A longstanding 6-to-3 circuit split exists on whether federal prisoners face a categorical bar under § 2244(b)(1) from raising any claim they previously presented in a successive § 2255 motion, in addition to the separate requirements of § 2255(h). The outcome determines whether federal prisoners face a stricter gatekeeping requirement than Congress may have intended.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar on relitigating previously raised claims under § 2244(b)(1) applies to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, imposing an additional gatekeeping requirement beyond § 2255(h).

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s bar does not apply to federal prisoners' § 2255 motions, and they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to election rules. The split affects whether such disputes can be resolved before elections or must wait until afterward, with significant implications for voting procedures nationwide.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates suffer a cognizable competitive injury sufficient for Article III standing when challenging election rules that may affect the outcome of their race.

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's speculative concern about the effect of an election rule on vote tallies is insufficient to establish the concrete injury required for Article III standing.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps Auer/Kisor deference to agency interpretations of ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, including Sentencing Guidelines.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the rule of lenity should prevail over deference to agency interpretations when sentencing guidelines or criminal statutes are ambiguous. The Third Circuit held that agency deference trumps lenity, while the Fourth, Fifth, and Eleventh Circuits hold that lenity prevails. This split affects how criminal defendants are sentenced under ambiguous guidelines provisions.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over Auer or Kisor deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through interpretation.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency deference (Auer/Kisor) applies even in the criminal sentencing context, allowing the Sentencing Commission's anti-defendant interpretations of ambiguous guidelines to control over the rule of lenity.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion is disqualifying).
splits.description: A deep circuit split exists on whether 'and' in § 3553(f)(1) means defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three listed criminal-history criteria, or if possessing any one is disqualifying. This split leads to dramatically different sentences for identical criminal histories depending on the circuit.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they satisfy all three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive or distributive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy within the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is entitled to deference when the guideline text does not mention inchoate offenses.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary expanding the career-offender guideline's definition of 'controlled substance offense' to include conspiracy and other inchoate offenses is entitled to controlling deference under Stinson/Kisor, or whether the unambiguous guideline text excludes such offenses. This split affects career-offender designations and substantially longer sentences for many defendants.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference — inchoate offenses included
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1's inclusion of inchoate offenses like conspiracy within the definition of 'controlled substance offense' is a permissible interpretation of an ambiguous guideline entitled to deference under Stinson.

positions.label: Commentary exceeds guideline text — inchoate offenses excluded
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously does not include inchoate offenses, and the commentary impermissibly expands the definition beyond the text and is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), may constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether courts may consider non-retroactive sentencing law changes as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release. Some circuits allow it, while others categorically bar it. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to § 1B1.13(b)(6) attempted to resolve this but has itself generated further disagreement about the Commission's authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in law, alone or in combination with other factors, may contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may not be considered
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, as this would circumvent Congress's decision not to make the change retroactive.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state schedule criminalizes a substance not listed on the federal controlled substances schedules.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the definition of 'controlled substance offense' under U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) is limited to offenses involving federally scheduled substances or extends to any substance controlled under state law. This affects career-offender and armed-career-criminal designations.

positions.label: State schedule controls — no federal limitation required
positions.summary: Holds that 'controlled substance offense' includes offenses under state law regardless of whether the substance is also federally scheduled, finding no textual basis to graft a federal law limitation onto the guideline.

positions.label: Federal schedule controls — substance must be federally listed
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if it involves a substance also controlled under federal law, ensuring national uniformity in sentencing enhancements.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's property belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value between filing and conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held it belongs to the estate, creating a split with other circuits that protect debtors' post-petition appreciation upon good-faith conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the broad definition of 'property of the estate' in § 541(a) means post-petition appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion, not the debtor.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A)'s reference to 'property of the estate, as of the date of filing' protects good-faith converting debtors from losing post-petition appreciation in their assets.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment in a criminal case is governed by the civil appeal deadline (30 days) or the criminal appeal deadline (14 days).
splits.description: Circuits have split on whether the Hyde Amendment, which allows criminal defendants to recover fees for frivolous prosecutions, triggers a civil or criminal appeal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies, but a minority of circuits apply the criminal deadline.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion arises directly from the criminal prosecution and is therefore governed by the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners challenging conditions of confinement or BOP decisions affecting the duration of custody must proceed via habeas corpus or may bring claims through Bivens or other civil rights mechanisms.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy for federal prisoners challenging BOP decisions that affect the timing of release (such as First Step Act time credits), or whether such claims may also be brought as civil rights actions. This affects the procedural options available to thousands of federal prisoners.

positions.label: Habeas is the exclusive remedy
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to BOP decisions affecting the fact or duration of confinement must be brought through habeas corpus and cannot be pursued through civil rights actions.

positions.label: Habeas is not the exclusive remedy
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to conditions of confinement or BOP administrative decisions may be cognizable through civil rights actions even when they affect the duration of custody, particularly when habeas relief would be inadequate.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish a defendant for the conduct underlying a supervised release violation, or whether revocation sentences must be justified solely by the breach of trust.
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit's precedent in United States v. Lewis allows courts to expressly punish defendants for the conduct underlying supervised release violations, placing it at odds with circuits that follow the Supreme Court's reasoning in Johnson v. United States limiting revocation sanctions to the breach of the court's trust.

positions.label: Courts may punish for underlying conduct
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider and punish the defendant for the specific criminal conduct underlying the supervised release violation when imposing a revocation sentence.

positions.label: Revocation sanctions limited to breach of trust
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release revocation sentences should be based on the defendant's breach of the court's trust inherent in the violation, not on punishing the underlying conduct itself.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, including the scope of liability and available remedies?
splits.description: The Supreme Court granted certiorari to review the Second Circuit's approach to legal standards governing discrimination claims in business establishments, indicating the circuit's approach may conflict with other circuits. The outcome could impact hospitality, retail, and service industries nationwide.

positions.label: Broader liability standards
positions.summary: Holds that discrimination claims against hospitality and business establishments should be evaluated under broader standards that provide greater protections and remedies to customers.

positions.label: Narrower liability standards
positions.summary: Holds that discrimination claims against business establishments should be evaluated under narrower standards that impose more limited obligations on business operators.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to' a broker's services, extends to common-law negligence claims arising from accidents during transportation arranged by the broker. This split has major implications for tort recovery in the trucking industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts state negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA's preemption clause bars state-law negligence claims against freight brokers because such claims are 'related to' the broker's services of arranging transportation.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt state negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that common-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted because they do not regulate the broker's prices, routes, or services within the meaning of the FAAAA.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Commission exceeded its statutory authority in promulgating U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6), which allows non-retroactive changes in law to serve as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release.
splits.description: The Seventh Circuit held that the Commission's 2024 policy statement allowing non-retroactive changes in law (like the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment) to serve as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release exceeds the Commission's statutory authority. Other circuits have applied the new policy statement as binding. This creates a split affecting prisoners serving stacked § 924(c) sentences.

positions.label: Commission exceeded its authority
positions.summary: Holds that U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6) is inconsistent with 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A) because it effectively makes non-retroactive sentencing changes retroactive, exceeding the Commission's delegated authority.

positions.label: Commission acted within its authority
positions.summary: Holds that the Sentencing Commission's policy statement in § 1B1.13(b)(6) is a valid exercise of the authority Congress delegated under 28 U.S.C. § 994(t) to define extraordinary and compelling reasons, and courts must follow it.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether FIFRA's preemption provision bars state tort claims that effectively require pesticide manufacturers to add warnings beyond what the EPA-approved label includes. This split affects product liability litigation involving widely used agricultural chemicals including Roundup.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because such claims impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' those under FIFRA.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law failure-to-warn claims are not preempted by FIFRA because they run parallel to federal requirements and do not impose additional labeling obligations.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims resembling habeas challenges — such as alleged trial errors or sentencing miscalculations — may serve as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits permit consideration of such factors while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used as a substitute for habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider legal errors or changes in law affecting a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release cannot be used as a vehicle to challenge the validity of a conviction or sentence, which must be addressed through habeas proceedings.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to define the class of 'transportation workers' exempt from the FAA under Section 1, particularly whether last-mile delivery workers who transport goods across state lines or distribute goods that have traveled in interstate commerce qualify for the exemption.

positions.label: Broad reading — delivery workers qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods that have moved in interstate commerce, including last-mile distributors of baked goods, are transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow reading — delivery workers do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that last-mile delivery workers are not 'transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the FAA because they do not work in the transportation industry.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
